title-group.article-title: The outcomes of Facial Artery Musculomucosal (FAMM) flaps in oral oncologic reconstructions

article-meta.aff: Department of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery Unit, Worcester Royal Hospital, Worcestershire Acute Hospitals NHS Trust, Worcester, United Kingdom

author-notes.corresp: Address for correspondence: Dr. Sumeet Raj, Oral & Maxillofacial / Head & Neck Surgery Unit, Queen Victoria Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, Holtye Road, East Grinstead, West Sussex RH19 3DZ, United Kingdom. E-mail: srajdocspapers@googlemail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright: © 2025 National Journal of Maxillofacial Surgery

license.license-p: This is an open access journal, and articles are distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 License, which allows others to remix, tweak, and build upon the work non-commercially, as long as appropriate credit is given and the new creations are licensed under the identical terms.

related-article.article-title: Retraction: The outcomes of Facial Artery Musculomucosal (FAMM) flaps in oral oncologic reconstructions

sec.title: Introduction:
sec.p: Head and neck cancer ablation results in challenging defects with complex reconstructive needs. The type of reconstruction depends on the nature, site, and size of the defect. Facial artery Musculomucosal (FAMM) flap reconstruction has emerged as a versatile option in a variety of small and medium-sized oral and oropharyngeal defects. We describe our experience reconstructing 12 intraoral defects with FAMM flap following resection for benign or malignant disease, with some having their surgery during the early Covid 19 pandemic.

sec.title: Materials and Methods:
sec.p: We examined patient demographics, diagnosis, site, TNM staging, type of FAMM flap, associated functional/cosmetic outcomes, oncological resection margins and adjuvant treatment.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: Most patients (75%) had Squamous cell carcinoma, with the palate (41.6%) being the most common site. Inferiorly based FAMM flaps (91.7%) were used in the majority. Clinically, T1 and T2 tumours (91.7%) were the most common. 91.7% had resumed normal dietary function; 83.4% had fully intelligible speech as assessed by the speech and language therapist; and 91.7% had aesthetically pleasing outcomes. 81.8% had clear resection margins. Two patients developed small residual oroantral fistulae requiring repair.

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: The FAMM flap is a reliable option for reconstructing small to medium-sized oral defects with several advantages and excellent functional and cosmetic outcomes.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Head and neck cancer ablation can result in challenging defects necessitating complex reconstruction to restore anatomical norm and function. The goals of reconstruction are to restore the oral cavity lining, maintain oral competence, facilitate speech and swallowing and produce an acceptable aesthetic result.

The type of reconstruction depends on the nature, site and size of the defect. Microvascular free tissue transfer (MFFT) has been a gold standard in the reconstruction of large defects, especially in the developed world. This is, however, technically demanding, expensive, time-consuming, and requires elaborate postoperative monitoring.[1]

The recent enormous challenges faced by head and neck oncology services worldwide during the Covid 19 pandemic prompted international consensus recommendations[2] with an increased emphasis on utilizing simpler pedicled flaps allowing enhanced recovery, early discharge and where possible to minimize operating times and perioperative Covid 19 risks.

Locoregional flaps, like Pectoralis major myocutaneous, Supraclavicular, and Submental island flaps, have been used for head and neck cancer defect reconstruction with various studies reporting their outcomes.[134]

More recently, several authors have reported good outcomes similar to free tissue transfer with the use of different locoregional flaps.[567] Nevertheless, despite advantages such as shorter operating times and early postoperative mobilization, these locoregional flaps are also reported to present numerous other problems, including lack of mucosal sensitivity, mobility, volume or optimal texture in the oral cavity.[8]

Facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap was designed more than 30 years ago as an axial pattern flap based on the facial artery by Pribaz et al.[9] with excellent results reported in a series of 15 patients. It is a well-vascularised composite soft tissue flap with robust venous drainage (from the submucosal venous plexus) and a wide arc of rotation, making it very versatile in intraoral reconstruction.

They can be harvested in a relatively short time, not requiring the usual two-surgeon approach often practiced for the microvascular reconstruction of head and neck defects. A very low donor-site morbidity and complication rates make FAMM flap an ideal option when reconstructing small and medium-sized oral defects.[1]
Despite the recent renewed interest in FAMM flaps,[1] it remains relatively uncommon, with very limited evidence of its application during the Covid 19 pandemic.[10]

sec.title: AIM
sec.p: We present our experience of performing 12 consecutive cases of FAMM flaps for the reconstruction of intraoral defects following ablative head and neck resection. This includes 4 cases performed in the early stages of the recent Covid 19 pandemic when there was an emphasis on shorter operating time and early discharge.

sec.title: MATERIALS AND METHODS
sec.p: A retrospective review of 12 FAMM flap cases between August 2017 and November 2020 was conducted and recorded on MS Excel.

Data was gathered about the diagnosis/indication for reconstruction, primary tumour site, size, TNM staging, type of FAMM flap reconstruction, functional and cosmetic outcomes, complications, need for salvage treatment and duration of hospital stay. We also recorded oncological resection margins and any relevant adjuvant treatments.

sec.title: Demographics
sec.p: Our patients had an average age of 66.9 years, with a majority being female (83.5%). The M:F ratio was 1:5.

sec.title: Diagnosis, site, primary tumour and node characteristics
sec.p: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) was the most common type of tumour (75%). Soft palate was the most common site (41.6%) and T1 and T2 were the most common tumour size (91.7%). The majority tumour resections were repaired with inferiorly based FAMM flaps (91.7%). Two patients also needed a buccal fat pad graft at the base of the defect to achieve closure. The results are presented in Tables 1 and 2.

tr.td: Lichenoid type chronic inflammation (SCC on Initial Biopsy)
Other (Non-Malignant-Initially cT2 SCC after Incisional biopsy results)

caption.p: Summary of patients with FAMM flap reconstructions

tr.td: Lichenoid type chronic inflammation. (SCC on initial Biopsy)
Posterior pharyngeal scarring & residual cleft; Associated anxiety about swallowing, Post-nasal drip. (Needed Pharyngoplasty)
Inferiorly based + Buccal fat pad repair
PO bleeding in neck & FOM, Hospital acquired Pneumonia (HAP).
Inferiorly based + Buccal fat pad repair

sec.p: The hospital stay ranged from 2-10 days with a mean duration of 5 days. Only 3 patients needed postoperative stay in the intensive care unit (ICU) for airway concerns, but this didn’t exceed 2 days (mean stay in ICU 1.6 days).

sec.title: Outcomes of FAMM Flap repair
sec.p: We measured both the aesthetic and the functional outcomes. The results are presented in Tables 3 and 4. The majority of the patients involved in this study (n = 11, 91.7%) had good aesthetic outcomes based on both patient opinion and surgeon assessment postoperatively.

caption.p: Aesthetic & Functional outcomes after FAMMF reconstructions
Functional outcomes following FAMMF Reconstructions- SALT Assessments and Dietary outcome

tr.td: Diet at last OMFS clinic FU

sec.p: Functional outcomes were assessed by the surgical team, complemented by speech and language therapy (SALT) input. The quality of speech and postoperative dietary intake was used as a measure of successful functional outcomes following surgery. Excellent speech with 95-100% intelligibility was achieved in the majority of the patients (n = 10, 83.4%).
The majority (66.7%, n = 8) of patients assessed by the SALT team had been discharged at 8 weeks postoperatively. At the last head and neck clinic follow-up, 91.7% (n = 11) had resumed a normal diet.

sec.title: Complications
sec.p: The complications included postoperative bleeding in the Floor of mouth (FOM)/Neck (n = 1), minor dehiscence (n = 2), small oroantral fistula (OAF), (n = 2), trismus (n = 1), mild sloughing (n = 1) and Hospital acquired Pneumonia (n = 1). Both OAF required surgical repair, with one of them needing a mobilization of the adjacent local tissue to repair the defect under local anaesthesia. One patient had posterior nasal drip and swallowing problems causing anxiety possibly due to a small residual cleft at the junction of the soft palate/FAMM flap and associated scarring of the posterior pharyngeal wall. This required a pharyngoplasty to resolve the problems.

sec.title: Oncological resection margins and need for adjuvant treatment
sec.p: 81.8% of malignant cases on final histology had clear resection margins (n = 9) and 18.2% had involved margins (n = 2). 36.5% had Postoperative Radiotherapy (PORT) (n = 4), while 54.5% didn’t need any PORT (n = 6). One patient wasn’t fit for PORT. The results are presented in Table 5.

sec.title: Duration of follow-up and outcomes
sec.p: The duration of follow-up ranged from 18-47 months (mean 32.75, median 36 months) with the majority (61.6%, n = 8) having a follow-up of over 25 months and half (50%) of the cohort followed up for more than 36 months. All patients were alive when the data was collected.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The Facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap is a versatile composite intraoral axial pattern flap, raised either superiorly or inferiorly based on the facial artery as its pedicle. It is made up of the mucosa, submucosa, part of the buccinator, along with the deeper plane of the orbicularis oris muscle [Figures 1-3]. The superiorly-based flap is pedicled on the angular artery (having a retrograde flow), while the inferiorly-based one is pedicled on the facial artery (with anterograde flow). It is primarily the location of the defect that dictates the type of flap used. The superiorly based flap is often used in the reconstruction of mucosal defects involving the anterior part of the hard palate, maxillary alveolus and antrum, upper lip, nasal septum and floor. On the contrary, the inferiorly based flap is preferred for posterior hard palate and alveolus, soft palate, tonsillar fossa, the floor of mouth (FOM), tongue, cheek and lower lip defects [Figures 4-7].[11112]

caption.p: Axial view of the cheek. The facial vein is located poster©iorly to the facial artery and is rarely included in the flap. 1: orbicularis oris; 2: facial artery; 3: facial vein; 4: buccinator muscle; 5: mandible; 6: superior pharyngeal constrictor. (Reprinted with permission-Ayad, T. (2019). Facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap. Operative Techniques in Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery, 30(2), 112–119)

Coronal view of the cheek and depiction of the layers included in the FAMM flap. 1: mucosa and submucosal layer; 2: buccinator muscle; 3: facial artery; 4: gingivo-buccal sulcus; 5: facial nerve motor branches; 6: facial expression muscles; 7: mandible; 8: molar tooth. (Reprinted with permission.-Ayad, T. (2019). Facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap. Operative Techniques in Otolaryngology - Head and Surgery, 30(2), 112–119). Neck Surgery, 30(2), 112–119.).

Landmarks for design of the inferiorly based FAMM flap. d: distance of 1 cm from the labial commissure delineates the anterior edge of the flap. L: largest width possible determined by the position of the orifice of Stensen’s duct. Inferiorly the base of the flap is centered over the area of the second and third molar teeth with a 2 cm base. 1: orifice of Stensen’s duct; 2: facial artery; 3: superior labial artery. (Reprinted with permission.- Ayad, T. (2019). Facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap. Operative Techniques in Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery, 30(2), 112–119. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.otot.2019.04.005)

Design of a Superiorly based FAMM flap. The landmarks are identical to the inferiorly based FAMM flap excluding the base of the flap which is located in the vicinity of the maxillary tuberosity or in the gingivolabial sulcus (blue circle). A distance of 0.5-1.0 cm is preserved between the posterior mar- gin of the flap and the gingiva (yellow star) to facilitate wound closure. The distal end of the flap is designed according to the size and shape of the defect. (Reprinted with permission-Ayad, T. (2019). Facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap. Operative Techniques in Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery, 30(2), 112–119).

Anatomy of the facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap. Both (a) inferiorly based and (b) superiorly based. (Reprinted with permission from Chang E, & Hanasono M. (2016). 31 Local Flaps in Head and Neck Reconstruction. In M. M. Hanasono, G. L. Robb, R. J. Skoracki, & P. Yu (Eds.), Reconstructive Plastic Surgery of the Head and Neck: Current Techniques and Flap Atlas (pp. 335–337). Georg Thieme Verlag. https://doi.org/10.1055/b-0036-134373)

(a) Superiorly based FAMM flap rotated and transposed for defects of the hard palate, alveolus, maxillary antrum, nose and upper lip. (b) Inferiorly based FAMM flap to reconstruct defects of the hard and soft palate, tonsillar fossa, alveolus, floor of the mouth and lower lip. (Reprinted with permission from Pribaz J, & Parrett B. (2017). 56 Facial Artery Musculomucosal Flaps. In K. Shokrollahi, I. S. Whitaker, & F. Nahai (Eds.), Flaps (pp. 618–620). Georg Thieme Verlag. https://doi.org/10.1055/b-0037-142130)

Superiorly based FAMM flap with facial artery attached to the muscle on the whole length of the flap. (Reprinted with permission-Ayad, T. (2019). Facial artery musculomucosal (FAMM) flap. Operative Techniques in Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery, 30(2), 112–119).

sec.p: The ability to harvest a flap with ease, along with the long length of the flap (8-9 cm) with a wide arc of rotation, makes this desirable for the reconstruction of small to medium-sized defects [Figures 8 and 9]. The surgical anatomy of the facial vessels, their branching pattern, and their relevance to the harvesting of a FAMM flap have been well described.[18913141516]

caption.p: An example of multi-subsite medium size defect reconstructed by FAMM flap. Upper panel: Defect and primary closure of the ventral tongue to use the FAMM flap in the reconstruction of the remaining subsites. Lower Panel: Defect following FAMM flap reconstruction. (Reprinted with permission from Ibrahim et al. Journal of Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery (2021) 50:67 https://doi.org/10.1186/s40463-021-00523-z)

Depiction of a typical moderate size defect of the oral cavity suitable for a FAMM flap reconstruction. Left panel: the defect involves 3 adjacent subsites including up to one third of the mobile tongue. Right panel: The tongue defect can be closed primarily or left to granulate while the FAMM flap covers the alveolar crest and floor of mouth defect. The donor site is closed primarily. (Reprinted with permission from Ibrahim et al. Journal of Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery (2021) 50:67https://doi.org/10.1186/s40463-021-00523-z)

sec.p: In contrast to previous papers, which have described the application of FAMM flaps for a variety of defects and pathologies,[11112171819] we report primarily the use of this flap for reconstructing intraoral defects following cancer resection. The majority of tumours (75%, n = 9) were Squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) with most of them being T1 and T2 lesions.
Although most of our resected tumours were clinically T1 or T2 tumours (91.7%), we were also able to use FAMM flaps in reconstructing one T4 lesion of the posterior maxilla and palate.

Massarelli et al.,[8] in their series had reconstructed 47 intraoral oncological defects using FAMM flaps and their modifications, the majority of them being T2 or T3 lesions and a smaller number of T4 tumours involving functionally challenging areas of the oral cavity. They were able to maintain the intrinsic mucosal sensitivity, volume, and mobility of the reconstructed defect, as well as the oral mucosal secretory function of the native tissue.[8]

Good functional outcomes were achieved based on various subjective tests (including 2-point tactile discrimination, heat and cold sensitivity) at 6 months postoperatively. In our series, the palate (and posterior maxilla) was the most commonly reconstructed region (50%) with the majority of the oncological reconstructions involving the soft palate (41.6%), which was in contrast to others’ experience of using FAMM flaps more commonly in Floor of mouth (FOM) and Tongue ablative defects.[1]

FAMM flaps have been extensively used in the repair of recurrent palatal fistulas of various sizes after primary cleft palate surgery with excellent functional results and minimal complications.[111220]

Ferrari et al.[11] reported using superiorly based FAMM flaps for anterior palatal and oronasal fistula repairs and routinely used forced mouth opening to avoid trauma from accidental biting in the first week. They divided the pedicle at 3 weeks under local anaesthesia.

sec.title: Type of FAMM flaps and modifications
sec.p: As per classical surgical technique,[9] the FAMM flap is harvested centred over the facial artery with an oblique orientation extending from the retromolar trigone to the level of the ipsilateral gingival labial sulcus near the alar margin. Over the years several modifications to the original technique have been described.[82122]

The original paper described the average width of the flap as 1.5 to 2 cm with the possibility of a flap as long as 8-9 cm being developed. Recent literature describes a width of up to 3 cm being developed, while still allowing primary closure of the donor site.[1]

In our cohort, inferiorly based FAMM flaps were used in the majority of the defects (91.7%), consistent with the anatomical location of most of our resections. Ayad and Xie[1] reported a smaller number (69%) of inferiorly based flaps, but the majority of the reconstructions involved the FOM.

The original FAMM flap reconstruction was a 2-stage operation, requiring a second surgery to divide the pedicle, especially in FOM reconstructions. There have been recent modifications to the technique to avoid the need for a second-stage procedure.[821] In the original technique, the inferiorly based flap was used in fully dentate patients, coming in from behind the maxillary tuberosity to reconstruct different parts of the palate. This, however, required use of a temporary bite block for 10 days and a second procedure for flap division/inset. We didn’t need to divide any of our FAMM flap pedicles. Ayad and Xie[1] reported an overall pedicle sectioning rate of 37% in FAMM flaps, with 62% of the FOM reconstructions needing a 2-stage procedure for pedicle division. Various modifications in harvesting techniques have been described,[2122] resulting in a decrease in the rate of revision procedures, but these apply to inferiorly based flaps used in FOM reconstructions.

Ibrahim et al.[22] compared traditional versus modified techniques of inferiorly based FAMM flaps. The overall reintervention rate dropped by half with similar functional results. The modified technique didn’t need any pedicle sectioning and consisted of using the pedicle to fill the posterior part of the FOM defect by extending the anterior incision over the alveolar crest, followed by subperiosteal dissection over the alveolar crest to reach the defect.

One of the biggest advantages of the FAMM flap is the ability to achieve direct closure of the donor site when the width of the defect is less than 3 cm. In case of wider defects, closure can be achieved with the use of a pedicled buccal fat pad graft harvested from the base of the flap and this undergoes rapid and spontaneous reepithelialisation.[1]

sec.title: Outcomes: Aesthetic outcomes
sec.p: Over the years, despite the increasing use of FAMM flaps, there has been a paucity of robust data about the aesthetic outcomes.[1] We had excellent outcomes in 91.7% of cases, which were based on both clinician assessment and patient opinion of their outcomes.

Joseph et al.[23] compared patient-reported aesthetics between islanded FAMM flaps and Fascio-cutaneous free flaps (FCFF) for lateral tongue tumours. They found that FAMM flaps were better (8.4/10) compared to FCFF (6/10) on the visual analogue scale (VAS).

In their analysis of functional and cosmetic outcomes, Jowett et al.[24] demonstrated a negligible impact of FAMM flap harvest on smile, lip depressor or elevator function. However, they emphasized the need for careful dissection to achieve these results as the mimetic muscles (Zygomaticus and lip elevators) are closely adherent to the plane of dissection and can be injured. FAMM flap harvesting may cause asymmetry with lip pucker and loss of ipsilateral lip height at rest to varying degrees.

sec.title: Functional outcomes
sec.p: We measured our functional outcomes by assessment of speech and postoperative and long-term ease of dietary intake. 75% of our cases were assessed by a Speech and Language Therapist (SALT) and most of them (66.7%) were discharged at 8 weeks postoperatively. 91.7% of our patients assessed by SALT had excellent speech with 95-100% intelligibility. There have been no previous studies reported with active input from the SALT team in assessing outcomes and providing support till discharge.
Ayad and Xie[1] conducted a systematic review of 441 FAMM flaps and found similar functional results, with 93% of the cases having a functional/understandable speech.

sec.title: Postoperative swallowing and dietary intake
sec.p: We surveyed our patients one week after surgery. 91.7% (n = 11) were eating and drinking at discharge, while 16.6% (n = 2) required nutritional supplements due to inadequate intake.

One patient was kept nil by mouth and had nasogastric feeding for one week. Postoperative dietary intake as a measure of function has been inconsistently recorded in most previous studies.[1] More commonly, dietary intake would commence 1 week after surgery, although it ranged from day 1 to 13 postoperatively.

sec.title: Complications
sec.p: Our overall complication rates were very low and similar to those (11.6%) reported by Massarelli et al.[8] in their cohort of 69 Myomucosal cheek flaps. In the largest systematic review on FAMM flaps[1] only a third of the papers analyzed had reported their complications (14 papers and a total of 376 FAMM flaps).

Their overall incidence of partial and complete flap necrosis was 12.2% and 2.9% respectively. The other complications reported were trismus, venous congestion, wound dehiscence, delayed scarring, tongue tethering, haematoma, bleeding from a facial artery, salivary fistula and osteoradionecrosis. Most of them were managed conservatively.

A similar complication profile was reported by Massarelli et al.,[8] as well as 3 instances of temporary facial/lower lip weakness due to marginal mandibular nerve paresis.[8] In our cohort, there were no FAMM flap losses, partial necrosis or reports of any temporary/permanent damage to facial nerve branches.

sec.title: Oncological resection margins and need for adjuvant treatment
sec.p: Surgical margins in Oral SCC (OSCC) resection are important prognostic indicators of recurrence and long-term survival and postoperative radiotherapy (PORT) has been shown to improve locoregional and disease-free survival in patients with locally advanced OSCC or with other adverse prognostic factors.[2526] 81.8% of our resections with the use of FAMM flaps for reconstruction had clear margins [Table 5] which was higher than our previous results (49% clear, 45% close and 6% involved) from an earlier retrospective review looking at 100 cases with head and neck cancer resections in our unit.[27] In a recent retrospective review of the treatment naïve 410 OSCC patients, the resection margins were not affected by the type of reconstruction used (Free or Pedicled flaps).[28]

sec.title: Hospital stay and follow-up
sec.p: Our mean duration of hospital stay (5 days) with FAMM flaps was about half the mean duration (8.4 days) associated with Radial forearm-free flaps in head and neck reconstruction.[29]
We had a robust follow-up period ranging 18-47 months (mean 32.8, median 36 months), which was much longer than previous studies.[830]

sec.title: FAMM flap use during Covid 19 pandemic
sec.p: The Covid 19 pandemic was an unprecedented health emergency and a time of uncertainty. This resulted in a widespread humanitarian crisis across the globe and placed huge pressure on the delivery of health care, including head and neck cancer services. Head and Neck Surgeons at the frontline had to adapt treatment protocols to meet changing needs to ensure optimal, safe and timely management of the oncology patients.[10]

Several national[31] and international consensus recommendations[2] provided guidance on different aspects of management including the emphasis on the use of Pedicled or local flaps in reconstruction to reduce the length of surgical time as well as limit usage of intensive care facilities at a time of heightened covid 19 hospital admissions during the pandemic.
We were quick to use our experience of using FAMM flaps to adapt to the changing situation during the pandemic and performed 4 cases with excellent results.

While there has been an ample evidence on the use of locoregional pedicled flaps in lieu of free tissue transfer during the pandemic,[56710] there is no detailed evidence on the use of FAMM flaps and their outcomes.

Rauso et al.[10] reported about the utilization of various local pedicled flaps during the pandemic in a cohort of 12 patients, which included 4 FAMM flaps in reconstructions of T1-2 lesions with excellent aesthetic and functional outcomes comparable to microvascular reconstruction. This resulted in a decrease in anaesthetic and operative times as well as intensive care and hospital stay of oncology patients at times of un-precedented restraints placed on the health service.

Janardhan et al. stressed that pedicled flaps like the Submental Island and FAMM flaps should be in the armamentarium of head and neck surgeons when the need arises. In their experience of 16 FAMM flaps, they reported equally good results compared to free flaps when time constraints were important factors in choosing the reconstruction method.[32] FAMM flaps can be an alternative to free tissue transfer in units with no microsurgical reconstruction facilities, including in low-resource regions of the world.[33]

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: The FAMM flap is a very versatile reconstructive option for small to medium-sized oral cavity defects following ablative resections. Over the last 3 decades, there has been a gradual increase in interest in the use of the FAMM flap in intraoral reconstruction as evidenced by a progressive increase in the number of papers published.[1] Several modifications of the original FAMM flap and their use for a wide range of indications provide promising alternative options to free tissue transfer, regional and distant pedicled flap reconstructions of oral defects with excellent aesthetic and functional results. Despite recent growing interest, the FAMM flap remains a rather sparingly used reconstructive option within most units in the UK and worldwide.

sec.title: Ethical considerations
sec.p: Ethical clearance or Institutional Review Board (IRB) clearance was not needed as it was a retrospective chart/ notes review. I have mentioned this in the journal submission questionnaire/ checklist at the time of original paper submission.

sec.title: Declaration of patient consent
sec.p: The authors declare that they have obtained consent from patients. Patients have given their consent for their images and other clinical information to be reported in the journal. Patients understand that their names will not be published and due efforts will be made to conceal their identity but anonymity cannot be guaranteed. Permission has been obtained from other publishers to use clinical photos from earlier peerreviewed publications and duly acknowledges in the paper.

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: There are no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: I am very grateful to my colleagues Mr. Sam Mattine, Consultant Oral and Maxillofacial/Head and Neck Reconstructive Surgeon and Ms. Elpida Samara, Worcester Royal Hospital, Worcestershire Acute Hospitals NHS Trust, Worcester for their invaluable help and support in various aspects of this manuscript and the study.
